strengths: Clear problem framing: truth maintenance in long-horizon memory
Novel combination of truth maintenance systems with LLM-based linking
Strong empirical results on abstention and single-session-user slices
Detailed ablation study isolating the linker's contribution
weaknesses: Overall accuracy is lower than the strongest baseline (61.3% vs 68.0%)
Limited evaluation scale (150-question slice of LongMemEval-S)
No statistical significance testing for main claims
Placeholder numbers and macros throughout the paper
Lack of comparison to state-of-the-art memory systems beyond A-Mem
required_revisions: Replace all placeholder macros (e.g., \FlatLMEAcc, \AmemLMEAcc) with actual numerical results
Provide statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for performance differences
Clarify the 150-question selection process and justify its representativeness
Include error bars or confidence intervals for all reported metrics
Expand related work to include more recent memory and truth maintenance systems
Justify hyperparameter choices (e.g., similarity thresholds, k values)

strengths: Novel application of truth maintenance to agent memory
Clear problem framing: truthfulness and abstention in long-horizon agents
Well-defined claim schema and graph structure
Strong focus on efficiency via session-batched writing and lexical gating
Explicit ablation study isolating the linker's contribution
weaknesses: Overall accuracy is lower than the strongest baseline (61.3% vs 68.0%)
No statistical significance testing reported for main comparisons
Small sample sizes for key slices (e.g., abstention n=9, SSU n=21)
Lacks detailed failure analysis for slices where performance regresses
Missing standard deviation or error bars for reported metrics
No comparison to other recent memory systems beyond A-Mem and flat RAG
required_revisions: Report standard deviations or confidence intervals for all accuracy metrics across seeds.
Perform and report statistical significance tests (e.g., McNemar) for all slice comparisons, not just abstention.
Clarify the sample selection process for the 150-question stratified slice and justify its representativeness.
Provide a detailed error analysis for slices where TTMG underperforms the baseline (e.g., overall, multi-session).
Include a full ablation study table with all question types, not just the four highlighted.
Specify the exact prompts and hyperparameters used for the LLM judge to ensure reproducibility.
Clarify the contradiction between the abstract (claims 100% on SSU) and Table 1 (claims 78.3% on KU). Ensure all numbers are consistent.

strengths: Clear problem framing: truth maintenance for long-horizon agents
Novel combination of claim-level memory with explicit contradiction/supersede edges
Practical design: session-batched writer reduces token cost vs. per-turn analysis
Strong empirical result on abstention slice (+44.4pp)
Ablation study isolates causal role of LLM linker
weaknesses: Overall accuracy (61.3%) is lower than flat baseline (68.0%)
Limited evaluation scale (150-question slice), small n for key slices (e.g., ABS n=9)
No latency/token breakdown between components (writer, linker, parser, reader)
No analysis of failure modes on slices where performance regresses
Placeholder numbers in macros (e.g., \AmemLMEAccABS 100.0) suggest incomplete results
required_revisions: Replace placeholder result macros (e.g., \AmemLMEAccABS 100.0) with actual final numbers
Provide per-component token and latency breakdown to justify efficiency claims
Clarify the 150-question slice selection method and justify its representativeness
Discuss the trade-off: gains on abstention/SSU vs. losses on other slices
Include error analysis for temporal-reasoning slice where TTMG did not improve
